name: Kingdom of Odoacer
note: Odoacer (Odovacer), a Germanic chieftain of Scirian/Hunnic origin, deposed the last Western Roman Emperor Romulus Augustulus on September 4, 476 — the conventional end-date of the Western Roman Empire. He ruled as King of Italy under nominal Eastern Roman suzerainty, maintaining Roman administrative structures. Defeated and killed by Theodoric the Ostrogoth at a peace banquet in Ravenna (493).

figures.name: Odoacer
figures.significance: Deposed Romulus Augustulus (476), ending the Western Roman Empire; sent the imperial regalia to Constantinople signalling that one emperor sufficed; ruled Italy competently for 17 years before Theodoric's treacherous assassination

name: Vandal Kingdom of North Africa
note: The Vandals crossed into North Africa in 429 under Gaiseric, having previously swept through Spain. They captured Carthage in 439, making it their capital. The Vandal fleet dominated the western Mediterranean and sacked Rome in 455. 'Vandalism' as a concept derives from their reputation, though they were no more destructive than other Germanic peoples. Destroyed by the Byzantine general Belisarius in 533 in a remarkably swift campaign.

figures.name: Gaiseric (Geiseric)
figures.significance: Greatest Vandal king; crossed into Africa with 80,000 people (429); built a naval empire; sacked Rome (455) — the second sack in 45 years; the only Germanic king to seriously challenge Roman naval supremacy

name: Weimar Republic
note: Germany's first democracy, proclaimed on November 9, 1918 as the Kaiser abdicated. Named after Weimar where the constitution was drafted. The republic faced hyperinflation (1923), the Great Depression (1929), political violence from left and right, and structural constitutional weaknesses (Article 48 emergency powers). Despite cultural brilliance (Bauhaus, Expressionism, cabaret), it was too fragile to survive the economic catastrophe. Hitler was appointed Chancellor on January 30, 1933.

policies.name: Article 48 Emergency Powers
policies.description: Allowed the President to rule by decree in emergencies without Reichstag consent. Used 136 times 1930–1932 as parliamentary government broke down — normalised authoritarian rule before Hitler.

figures.name: Friedrich Ebert
figures.significance: First President; Social Democrat who proclaimed the Republic; made the fateful deal with the military (Ebert-Groener Pact) that preserved the old officer corps — the army that would later undermine democracy

figures.name: Gustav Stresemann
figures.significance: Chancellor then Foreign Minister; stabilised the currency; negotiated the Dawes Plan and Locarno Treaties; brought Germany back into international society; Nobel Peace Prize 1926; his death removed the Republic's ablest defender

name: Czechoslovakia
note: Created from the Habsburg collapse at Versailles — the most successful democratic state in interwar Central Europe. Munich Agreement (1938) forced it to cede the Sudetenland to Hitler; fully occupied 1939. Liberated 1945; Communist coup 1948. The Prague Spring (1968) — Alexander Dubček's 'socialism with a human face' — was crushed by Soviet tanks. Velvet Revolution (1989) ended communism peacefully. Velvet Divorce (1993) split into Czech Republic and Slovakia.

policies.name: Prague Spring
policies.description: Dubček's liberalisation programme — free press, political pluralism, economic reform. Crushed by Warsaw Pact invasion (August 20–21, 1968); 'normalisation' followed. The definitive demonstration that Soviet-style socialism could not be reformed from within.

figures.name: Tomáš Garrigue Masaryk
figures.significance: Philosopher-president who created Czechoslovakia through diplomacy at Versailles; a genuine liberal democrat who built Central Europe's most successful interwar state; his son Jan was defenestrated after the Communist coup

figures.name: Alexander Dubček
figures.significance: Led the Prague Spring — 'socialism with a human face'; removed by Soviets; worked as a forest ranger for 20 years; returned as Speaker of Parliament after the Velvet Revolution

figures.name: Václav Havel
figures.significance: Playwright turned dissident turned president; led the Velvet Revolution (1989); first post-communist President; the most admired European political figure of his generation
